rationale: In the theory development, the citation is used to support central premises (leisure as intrinsically motivated, work-like framing as effortful/chore-like, and lower utility under work framing), not just a passing literature list. The authors explicitly position their contribution against this prior framing research by arguing that a scheduling manipulation can produce similar work-like effects for unambiguous leisure tasks. Because this cited paper helps ground the conceptual setup and hypothesis logic, the citation function is substantive and foundational.
relation_description: The citing paper uses Choi and Fishbach (2011) as part of the core theoretical base for distinguishing work-like versus leisure-like construal and for linking work construal to lower utility. It then builds its own argument that scheduling can induce a work-like construal even for clearly leisure activities.
